land.subtitle_main: "A shot to dream the world in which we`d want to live"
land.land.intro_quote: Visiting a Robert Doisneau`s exhibition it`s kinda like travelling through time without the need of a complex machine: only sight is needed. And if Pordenone is the frame, the experience becomes even more intimate and suggestive.

home.hero_quote: "There are days in wich the simple act of seeing seems to be true happiness"

interviews.title: What do our visitors say?
interviews.q1: "I've noticed that some titles are in contrast with the shot's title. Did you get influenced by them or have you prefered to just watch the photos?"
interviews.a1: "I rarely read the titles, because I fix myself more on the details of the photos or paintings."
interviews.q2: "Why di you choose to come see this exhibition?"
interviews.a2: "I'm here with my students for an internal study project."
interviews.q3: "Is there a photo that captured you the most?"
interviews.a3: "Yes! The title is 'Anita', because it's my mother's name."
interviews.q4: "Why di you choose to come see this exhibition?"
interviews.a4: "Actually, I've already visited it in Verona, but I decided to to come back because I think that experiencing Doisneau it's always a good choise."
interviews.q5: "Why di you choose to come see this exhibition?"
interviews.a5: "Doisneau has always been one of my favourite photographers, but particulary in recent years he became even more important, since I've done multiple photos of scolars and having recently opened a B&B in an old school, I decided to expose some of his photos and that makes me feel more bonded to this artist."
interviews.q6: "Is there a photo that captured you the most?"
interviews.a6: "One that caught me the most I think it's called "Puppies on the leash", that I've never sawn before."
interviews.q7: "Do you now have any interests in taking in hand a camera, and start taking photos of landscapes or subjects?"
interviews.a7: "I'm from another generation, so I've taken in hand a camera before, and I've used it for a long time. Now I'm too more lazy and so i use my phone. My mother also used to develop photos herself."
interviews.q8: "Do you now have any interests in taking in hand a camera, and start taking photos of landscapes or subjects?"
interviews.q9: "I've noticed that some titles are in contrast with the shot's title. Did you get influenced by them or have you prefered to just watch the photos?"
interviews.a9: "No, usually I watch the photo first. But I've noticed some captions that are very funny in contrast with the photos (cit: Hommages Respectueux / Respectable Homages - 1954"

about.bio_p1: Robert Doisneau was not just a photographer, but a poet of the everyday. Born in Gentilly, he dedicated his life to capturing beauty in the small things of post-war Paris, calling himself a "fisher of images" rather than a hunter.
about.bio_p2: His photography is imbued with irony, tenderness, and a deep love for human beings, making him one of the greatest exponents of French humanist photography.
about.highlight_1: 1934: Starts working for Renault as an industrial photographer.
about.highlight_2: 1944: Documents the Liberation of Paris from the barricades.
about.highlight_3: 1950: Takes his most famous shot, Le Baiser de l'hôtel de ville.
about.highlight_4: 1984: Named Knight of the Legion of Honor.
about.quote: "What I was trying to show was a world where I would feel good, where people would be kind, where I would find the tenderness I hoped to receive. My photos were proof that this world can exist."
about.cta_text: Discover the curated selection for the "Road to Doisneau" exhibition.

articles.art1_title: Paris on the Noncello: Robert Doisneau arrives in Pordenone
articles.art1_desc: The complicit gaze of the photographer who made the ordinary eternal arrives in the city with a unique selection.
articles.art1_subtitle: A journey into the soul of French humanist photography through the streets of the historic center.
articles.art1_p1: Pordenone transforms into a movie set from another era...
articles.art2_title: Strolling with Doisneau: What to do in Pordenone
articles.art2_desc: How to turn a cultural day into a "humanist" experience among the porches and taverns of the historic center.
articles.art3_title: Le Baiser de l'Hôtel de Ville 1950
articles.art3_desc: Behind Robert Doisneau's most famous shot lies a story of actors, trials, and "fake" spontaneity.
articles.art4_desc: If Le Baiser represents passionate love, "La Dernière Valse" is the manifesto of poetic melancholy.
articles.art5_title: Pordenone spellbound by 'The Kiss': Overwhelming success
articles.art5_desc: Full rooms and queues outside museums for the photographer of happiness.
articles.art6_title: One more month of photography: Exhibition extended
articles.art6_desc: Due to extraordinary attendance, there is time until August 14.

cosaFarePordenone.art2_title: Going around with Doisneau: What to do in Pordenone
cosaFarePordenone.art2_subtitle: How to transform a cultural day in a “humanistic” experience between porticoes and taverns of the historic center.
cosaFarePordenone.art2_p1: After having nourished the eyes with Robert Doisneau`s works, the excitement yearns to be taken outside, in the city streets. Pordenone, with the elegant porticoes and medieval alleys, lends itself to be lived with the same “humanistic” curiosity of the french photographer.
cosaFarePordenone.art2_p2: Start your stroll in Corso Vittorio Emanuele II. Here, the kind invitation is to forget the smartphone in your pocket: observe the passerby-faces, the lights and shadow games underneath the painted arches and the slow rhythm of steps on the cobbled streets. It`s in these ordinary details that Robert Doisneau would have found “his case music”.
cosaFarePordenone.art2_quote: "What I was trying to show was a world where I would have felt better, where people would have been gentler, where I would have found the sweetness I hoped to receive."
cosaFarePordenone.art2_p3: Not a day in Pordenone could be declared finished without having made a stop in the historic city-center taverns. Walk into a typical local tavern, order a “tajut” of wine and listen to the buzz of conversations. In that sincere conviviality, between a stolen smile from a balcony and a gesture of understanding, you`ll find again the spirit of the Parisian bistrot that Doisneau really loved to go to.
cosaFarePordenone.art2_p4: Finally, reach the Noncello shore at sunset, The light fog that frequently rises from the water that resembles the suffused atmosphere of a morning alongside the Senna, perfectly closing the circle of a day dedicated to the real beauty.

valse.art3_detail_title: La Dernière Valse: The last breath of the party
valse.art3_h2_1: 1. The beat of the 14th of July
valse.art3_p1: While Paris was celebrating the Storming of the Bastille, Doisneau decided to distance himself from the fireworks. In an alley corner. He found a couple who danced alone, while the musicians were starting to put away. It`s the portrait of the “end of the party”, that magic moment where the public joy was starting to turn to private intimacy.
valse.art3_p2: Unlike “the Kiss”. Here there`s no staging. Doisneau loved to define himself a fisher: he played the waiting game until reality composed itself the masterpiece.  He noticed the grace of the two dancers on the humid paving and he shot, capturing not only a dance, but the desire of an entire generation to go back to smiling after the dark years of war.
valse.art3_quote: "“There`s a sort of grace in seeing a party till the end. It`s the moment in which the masks drop and only humanity remains.”"
valse.art3_p3: What makes this shot eternal and equilibrated. The simple dress of the woman, the streetlamps that reflect the light on the cement and the dignified hug. It`s not a noble dance, but the walzer of the common people that, for one night, transforming the periferic Paris in the world's most prestigious stage.

basier.art4_detail_title: Le Basier de l`Hôtel de Ville: Chronicle of an emotional construct
basier.art4_p1: For decades we dreamed of this shot, believing it was the peak of spontaneity. The truth is more staged: Doisneau did not “steal” the moment, he directed it. He noticed Françoise and Jacques in a bistrot and, enchanted by their chemistry, he asked them to replicate that kiss in front of the Hotel de Ville for a Life service. It was not news. It was vision.
basier.art4_p2: In the 90s, the mith risked breaking under the pressure of the judicial system. A lot of couples argued to have been the real protagonists, trying to get compensation in the millions. Doisneau was forced to confess: his photo was staged. Françoise Bornet appeared with the original print signed by the maestro, the only proof that put an end to the disputes.
basier.art4_quote: "“I would have never permitted myself to shoot at people kissing on the street. Two lovers kissing are rarely a legitimate one”"
basier.art4_p3: In 2005, the original copy of Françoise was sold at auction for a record figure. Over 150 thousand euros for a piece of paper. Why? Because even knowing that it was a staged pose, that kiss continued to represent the Paris that all of us want to dream about: free, romantic and timeless.

parigiNoncello.art1_title: Paris on the Noncello: Robert Doisneau arrives in Pordenone
parigiNoncello.art1_subtitle: A trip in the soul of french humanistic photography between the streets of the storic citycenter.
parigiNoncello.art1_p1: Pordenone transforms itself in a cinematic set of other times. With the arrival of the exhibition dedicated to Robert Doisneau, the Friulian city welcomes the delicacy of a look that was capable of stopping time without breaking the enchantment.
parigiNoncello.art1_p2: Don`t expect plastic poses or constructed poses. Doisneau was an “image fisher”, a man capable of waiting hours the perfect alignment between radiant light and the smile of a passerby
parigiNoncello.art1_quote: "“What I was trying to show was a world where I would have felt better, where people would have been gentler”"
parigiNoncello.art1_p3: Our set-up has been tough to create a continuous dialogue between the photos and Pordenone`s architecture. Strolling between the halls it`s like walking down the Senna, an experience that bonds the Parisian white and black with the colors of our porticoes.

find_us.reach_text: The exhibition is collocated at Harry Bertoia Gallery, in the heart of Pordenone. Easily reachable from the station (10-minutes walk) or by car using the nearby "multilayer parking lot”.
